title: Wire Weld Defect Diagnosis and Stick Penetration
summary: These pages cover troubleshooting common wire weld defects including porosity, excessive spatter, crooked beads, and burn-through. They also address stick weld heat control and penetration adjustment, with example weld diagrams illustrating current, speed, and arc length effects.
key_facts: Porosity can be caused by incorrect polarity, insufficient shielding gas, dirty workpiece, or inconsistent travel speed.
Excessive spatter that is grainy and large indicates a problem; fine spatter is normal.
Incorrect polarity causes both porosity and excessive spatter in wire welding.
CTWD too long can cause porosity, excessive spatter, and crooked or wavy beads.
Burn-through occurs when base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.
To fix burn-through, reduce current and/or wire feed speed, and increase travel speed.
Crooked or wavy beads can result from inaccurate welding, inconsistent travel speed, or excessive CTWD.
Use two hands or rest hand on a steady surface to improve welding accuracy.
Increasing current and welding more slowly increases workpiece heat and penetration.
Decreasing current and welding more quickly reduces workpiece heat and limits penetration.
Stick welds have a coat of slag over them until cleaned; clean welds first.
Shielding gas issues should be addressed by increasing flow, cleaning the nozzle, and maintaining proper CTWD.
